metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion explicitly stating that a review containing any indeterminate prerequisite coverage record is emitted as unsigned INDETERMINATE rather than a signed PASS, distinct from AC 18's per-record assertion.
evidence: 'complete prerequisite coverage is required for certification, so any indeterminate record yields an unsigned INDETERMINATE review rather than signed PASS'
AC 18: 'Every readable prerequisite receives one three-disposition coverage record' — tests the record, not the review-level consequence
No AC reads: 'A review with any indeterminate prerequisite coverage record is unsigned INDETERMINATE'
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion covers the critical rule that ANY indeterminate PrerequisiteCoverageRecord causes the whole review to emit unsigned INDETERMINATE rather than a signed PASS. AC 18 only tests that each readable prerequisite receives a coverage record; it does not test the whole-review disposition consequence.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (no matching AC); Settled Design > Signed plan-material pins: 'any indeterminate record yields an unsigned INDETERMINATE review rather than signed PASS'
scenarios: A test suite verifies that each prerequisite gets a three-disposition record (AC 18 passes) but never asserts that the whole review outcome is unsigned when any record is indeterminate — the critical gate behavior goes untested.
An implementer reads AC 18 and concludes that indeterminate records are captured but the review may still be signed, missing the certification-blocking consequence.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'A review with one or more indeterminate prerequisite coverage records is emitted unsigned with disposition INDETERMINATE and cannot be used to satisfy the close gate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether a too-large singleton prerequisite emits a `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord(disposition=indeterminate, reason_code='evaluation-error')` (satisfying the one-record-per-prerequisite AC) or triggers a top-level unsigned too-large outcome that short-circuits the entire prerequisite review, and reconcile this with AC 'Every readable prerequisite receives one three-disposition coverage record'.
evidence: Plan: 'A singleton escalates through models_at_or_above(); if it still exceeds the largest window, emit too-large remediation'
Plan: 'Oversized input likewise yields unsigned too-large remediation' (in the Signed plan-material pins section, referring to the whole review)
Plan: 'Every readable prerequisite receives one three-disposition coverage record' (AC)

The plan never states whether 'too-large remediation' for a singleton prerequisite produces a PrerequisiteCoverageRecord(disposition=indeterminate, reason_code=...) or exits the coverage-record loop entirely
findings.finding: The plan specifies that a singleton prerequisite that exceeds the largest model window emits 'too-large remediation', but never specifies whether this produces a PrerequisiteCoverageRecord with disposition=indeterminate (so the per-prerequisite list is complete) or a separate top-level artifact outside the coverage-record list. The plan says 'any indeterminate record yields an unsigned INDETERMINATE review' — if too-large remediation bypasses the PrerequisiteCoverageRecord schema entirely, the propagation to unsigned INDETERMINATE is broken for this case.
findings.location: Settled design > Prerequisite consistency review (sizing / model-ladder escalation fallback)
scenarios: A single very large prerequisite causes model-ladder exhaustion: implementor is unsure whether to emit PrerequisiteCoverageRecord(disposition=indeterminate, reason_code='evaluation-error') or to abort the entire review with a top-level too-large outcome
AC 'Every readable prerequisite receives one three-disposition coverage record' is violated if too-large exits without emitting a record

evidence: tests/golden/config_surface.json: 145e-52a9-26e3-4209 & 75de-02b0-d454-410b (no ordering edge)
findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the schema state of `plan_review_phase` in compiled state after rollback when old code performs a full re-reduction from raw events (not just snapshot pass-through), and document whether phase-history correctness is preserved or must be re-derived from status in that scenario.
evidence: 'process_snapshot restores unknown compiled-state keys and compaction drops only its explicit strip set, so an older reducer must pass plan_review_phase through unchanged on re-compaction'
'To remove the 0.80 execution-review behavior, first disable both gates, then deploy the preceding code version'

ABSENCE: the plan does not address schema state when the deployed old code performs a reducer-cache version bump (forcing full re-reduction from raw events, which old code cannot produce plan_review_phase from)
findings.finding: The rollback path asserts that 'process_snapshot restores unknown compiled-state keys' and 'compaction drops only its explicit strip set', so plan_review_phase passes through old-code re-compaction unchanged. However, the plan does not specify the schema state if old code is deployed post-rollback AND performs its own reducer-cache version bump (re-reducing from raw events). In that scenario, old code would not derive plan_review_phase from events, leaving the field absent. The next forward-upgrade would then re-bootstrap from compiled status rather than from preserved phase history — potentially incorrectly classifying execution-phase tickets (e.g., a 'blocked' ticket that was set to execution) as 'execution' or 'planning' based only on status, without regard for the actual phase transition history.
findings.location: Settled design § Enforcement and rollout — rollback paths; AC: 'The plan_review_phase projection survives compaction'
scenarios: Post-rollback, an operator bumps the old reducer cache version to fix an unrelated bug — old reducers re-derive all compiled state from raw events, losing plan_review_phase; on re-upgrade, tickets are re-bootstrapped from status rather than preserved phase, potentially mis-classifying some tickets' review phase.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a rollback note stating: 'If old code performs a reducer-cache version bump post-rollback, plan_review_phase will be absent after re-reduction; on re-upgrade, bootstrap re-derives from compiled status as for pre-feature snapshots. Operators should avoid a cache version bump in the rollback window, or accept that phase may re-bootstrap from status rather than history.' Additionally, consider including phase-bearing STATUS events in the raw-event stream so that re-reduction from events can reconstruct phase without relying solely on snapshot pass-through.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate in 5967 that it owns updating tests/unit/test_attestation_invalidation.py to cover phase_status in DerivedPlanReviewHealth, and that 75de's additive-health assertions use an open (not exact-shape) contract to survive 5967's extension.
evidence: 75de AC: 'tests/unit/test_attestation_invalidation.py proves existing plan-review validity keys retain their values with additive health'
75de: 'Child 5967 extends the same contract with phase_status and phase compatibility after it introduces the phase manifest format'
5967 AC: 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth phase_status is exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed under the documented planning/execution comparison'
5967 AC: 'Phase incompatibility is enforced consistently by claim, close profile, reuse, re-sign, audit, and health'
5967 Verification section does not mention test_attestation_invalidation.py
findings.finding: 75de-02b0-d454-410b writes tests/unit/test_attestation_invalidation.py asserting the DerivedPlanReviewHealth shape and asserting 'existing plan-review validity keys retain their values with additive health,' yet 5967-4476-7f9f-442d subsequently extends that same DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract by adding phase_status — neither child designates ownership of updating test_attestation_invalidation.py after 5967's extension, leaving 75de's assertions potentially stale once 5967 is merged.
scenarios: After 5967 merges, test_attestation_invalidation.py may contain DerivedPlanReviewHealth assertions that omit phase_status, passing falsely because the schema is tested without the field 5967 adds
cb6b renders phase_status alongside pin_status; if test_attestation_invalidation.py never validates their co-presence, cb6b's rendering tests may test against an incomplete fixture contract
If 75de's additive-health assertion is an exact shape check, adding phase_status in 5967 may break 75de's test without a documented fix path
findings.suggested_fix: In 5967's Acceptance Criteria or Verification section, add: 'Update tests/unit/test_attestation_invalidation.py to assert DerivedPlanReviewHealth.phase_status is present and correctly valued alongside pin_status.' In 75de, document that the health shape assertion accepts extra keys so 5967's phase_status addition does not break it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact triggering condition for `malformed-pin` in TargetPinDetail.pin_status — specifically, state whether it can only arise from a top-level malformed review (in which case TargetPinDetail is never populated) or from a post-parse logical error (e.g., a pin referencing a role that conflicts with the relationship graph), and reconcile this with the ManifestFormatError-to-top-level-malformed path.
evidence: "New parsers raise one ManifestFormatError on duplicate, malformed, unknown-enum, missing-token, extra-token, or invalid numeric lines"
"compute_validity parses all three fields together inside one narrow ManifestFormatError catch and returns malformed/invalid"
"Malformed manifest syntax remains the shared malformed review state"
TargetPinDetail pin_status enum includes `malformed-pin` alongside `current`, `stale-pin-drift`, `stale-pin-missing`
ABSENCE: no plan text describes a scenario where a pin line survives manifest parsing yet still yields TargetPinDetail.pin_status=malformed-pin
findings.finding: The `malformed-pin` value in TargetPinDetail.pin_status is not specified to be distinct from the top-level `malformed/invalid` compute_validity result: if ManifestFormatError is raised for any malformed pin line and immediately caught to return top-level malformed/invalid, the health-contract path (TargetPinDetail evaluation) is never reached, making `malformed-pin` unreachable or its triggering conditions undefined.
findings.location: Settled design — Enforcement and rollout: DerivedPlanReviewHealth / TargetPinDetail schema
scenarios: An implementer writing the TargetPinDetail evaluation loop has no defined triggering condition for `malformed-pin` and must invent one, risking divergence from the intended model.
An audit or UI consumer receiving `malformed-pin` from TargetPinDetail cannot distinguish it from a top-level malformed review, breaking the 'shared derived-health contract' AC.
findings.suggested_fix: If every syntactically or semantically malformed pin line is caught as ManifestFormatError and surfaced as top-level malformed/invalid (with no TargetPinDetail produced), remove `malformed-pin` from the TargetPinDetail.pin_status enum and document that TargetPinDetail is only populated for syntactically valid manifests. Alternatively, define a specific post-parse logical error (e.g., a pin whose role enum is valid but conflicts with current relationship graph membership) that produces TargetPinDetail with pin_status=malformed-pin while the top-level valid field still reflects normal drift evaluation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the packed-evaluation AC self-contained by embedding its four measurable terms: state that packed recall must be no lower than the one-per-call baseline, packed false acceptance no higher, every prerequisite must receive a coverage record, and attribution errors must be zero.
evidence: AC text: 'Packed prerequisite evaluation matches the one-per-call fidelity contract'

Plan body defines the contract as: 'packed recall must be no lower, packed false acceptance no higher, every prerequisite must receive a record, and attribution errors must be zero' — only in the Prerequisite consistency review section
ABSENCE: the AC does not embed any of these four measurable terms
findings.finding: AC 'Packed prerequisite evaluation matches the one-per-call fidelity contract' is not self-contained: it references 'the fidelity contract' whose measurable terms (packed recall no lower, packed false acceptance no higher, every prerequisite receives a record, attribution errors zero) are defined only in the plan body, not in the AC itself, making the AC non-independently evaluable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Packed prerequisite evaluation matches the one-per-call fidelity contract'
scenarios: A reviewer checking AC completion cannot determine pass/fail from the AC line alone without hunting through the plan body for the contract definition.
The fidelity contract could be amended in the plan body without the AC reflecting the change, creating a silent divergence.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC line with: 'Packed prerequisite evaluation produces: recall no lower than the one-per-call gold baseline, false acceptance no higher than baseline, a coverage record for every prerequisite, and zero attribution errors; failures revert to one-per-call fallback.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion asserting that a review attempt with any unresolvable or unreadable pinned target (missing, deleted, ambiguous reference, reducer error, or canonical-ID mismatch) aborts before any LLM call and returns an appropriate error disposition.
evidence: 'Missing/deleted targets, ambiguous or malformed references, reducer errors, or canonical-ID mismatch are unresolved/unreadable and stop review before any LLM call'
No AC covers pre-LLM abort on unresolvable targets; the nearest candidates (AC 4 on pre-sign re-collection abort, AC 18 on readable prerequisite records) test different behaviors
findings.finding: The design rule that unresolvable or unreadable prerequisite/child targets (missing, deleted, ambiguous, reducer-error, or canonical-ID mismatch) abort the review before any LLM call is described in the plan body but has no corresponding acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (no matching AC); Settled Design > Signed plan-material pins: 'Missing/deleted targets, ambiguous or malformed references, reducer errors, or canonical-ID mismatch are unresolved/unreadable and stop review before any LLM call'
scenarios: An implementer proceeds to an LLM call even when a prerequisite reference is ambiguous, because no AC demands a pre-LLM gate; the error surfaces later as a malformed attribution rather than a clean early abort.
A reviewer testing the plan cannot find an AC to write a failing test against for unresolvable-target behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align AC 16 terminology with the plan body by replacing 'compatible review' with 'current certified plan review with a compatible phase', matching the body's 'current certified plan review' language.
evidence: AC 16: 'Enabled close enforcement requires a separately earned compatible review'
Body: 'When enabled, task, story, and epic close operations require a current certified plan review'
Body: 'Close checks signature, compatible phase, the ticket's own material, relationship pins, and other non-code attestation validity'
'compatible' in the close section specifically means phase-compatible (planning or execution with floor ≥ 0.80), but 'compatible review' in AC 16 could be interpreted more narrowly
findings.finding: AC 16 uses the term 'compatible review' while the plan body uses 'current certified plan review' for the close-gate requirement. 'Compatible' in the AC is ambiguous — it could be read as phase-compatibility only, omitting signature validity and material freshness checks that the body specifies are also required.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC 16: 'Enabled close enforcement requires a separately earned compatible review'; Settled Design > Execution reviews and close gate: 'require a current certified plan review'
scenarios: A test for AC 16 asserts only that the phase is compatible and misses signature validity or material freshness checks, leaving those paths uncovered.
An implementer reading only the AC implements a weaker gate (phase check only) without the full certification check the body demands.
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC 16 to: 'Enabled close enforcement requires a current certified plan review with a compatible phase (planning, or execution with floor ≥ 0.80) earned separately before the close operation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit verification step (test or code reference) confirming the existing compaction implementation passes unknown compiled-state keys through, OR add an expand-contract sequence that ships the pass-through guarantee before shipping plan_review_phase, so rollback safety is proven rather than assumed.
evidence: 'an older reducer must pass plan_review_phase through unchanged on re-compaction rather than strip it'
'Compatibility tests run old reduction plus re-compaction against a phase-bearing snapshot, then roll forward and prove the exact phase value survives without bootstrap re-derivation or corruption'
No expand-contract step (e.g., first ship the pass-through, then ship the feature) is described to guarantee the assumption holds before the feature ships.
findings.finding: The rollback path relies on an unverified assertion about existing code: 'an older reducer must pass plan_review_phase through unchanged on re-compaction rather than strip it.' If the existing compaction implementation strips unknown compiled-state keys (a common defensive pattern), rolling back to the prior binary after deployment would silently discard the plan_review_phase projection from any snapshot compacted post-rollback, potentially corrupting phase state without error. The plan asserts this as a fact but provides no verification mechanism or fallback.
findings.location: Enforcement and rollout section — 'process_snapshot restores unknown compiled-state keys and compaction drops only its explicit strip set'
scenarios: A rollback is performed after plan_review_phase is stored in compacted snapshots; the older reducer strips unknown state keys; all compacted tickets silently lose their phase; the next re-review bootstraps from status heuristic rather than the correct history-derived value.
A future compaction refactor strips unknown keys as a cleanup, violating the silent assumption and breaking rollback safety retroactively.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit compatibility test that runs the current (pre-feature) compaction code against a snapshot containing an unknown key and asserts it is preserved, making the assertion load-bearing and CI-enforced rather than prose-only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name a specific doc artifact (file path or ADR ID) for (a) the execution-phase/0.80-floor architectural decision, (b) both new config flags with their interaction semantics, and (c) the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract shape consumed by all detailed surfaces.
evidence: 'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation' — no file path or ADR number cited
'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config' — no ADR recording this decision
DerivedPlanReviewHealth schema defined only in the plan body with no doc artifact reference
Two new config flags introduced with no config-reference doc update cited
findings.finding: The execution-phase / 0.80 priority-floor policy is described as 'a fixed protocol risk policy' — a significant architectural decision — but no ADR or named doc artifact is created for it. The two new operator-facing config flags (`verify.enforce_plan_material_pins`, `verify.require_plan_review_for_close`) and the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` contract (consumed by CLI, MCP, audit, web UI) are only vaguely pointed to 'advanced attestation/gate documentation' without naming a specific document, file path, or doc PR.
findings.location: Health and documentation section; Execution reviews and close gate section; Enforcement and rollout section
scenarios: An operator enabling enforcement cannot find documentation for what enforce_plan_material_pins does or its interaction with require_plan_review_for_close.
A future contributor questions why 0.80 was chosen; there is no ADR to consult, so the rationale is rediscovered (or misunderstood) from plan prose.
A consumer implementing the MCP detail endpoint cannot find the canonical DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract definition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly identify all existing attestation/manifest-format documentation that documents the manifest line set or order and mark them for update as part of this plan's deliverables.
evidence: The new manifest order is specified in detail (plan-review, ticket, material, model, runner, blocking, advisory, review-phase, optional priority-floor, rebar-version, …, zero or more plan-material-pin lines…)
'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation' — implies existing attestation docs exist but doesn't flag them for update
No existing doc file is listed for update in the plan's scope or acceptance criteria
findings.finding: The manifest format changes materially — execution reviews now require a `review-phase` line and new `plan-material-pin` lines are added — but the plan does not identify which existing attestation/manifest-format documentation becomes stale and needs updating. Any current doc that describes the complete manifest line order or the closed set of manifest fields would be invalidated.
findings.location: Settled design — 'The final deterministic manifest order is…' and Health and documentation section
scenarios: An integrator reads the existing attestation format reference, builds a parser based on the documented closed manifest line set, and fails to handle review-phase or pin lines because the doc was never updated.
A developer writing a new manifest verifier uses stale documentation and omits the new lines, producing a broken verifier that passes review.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a measurement step (e.g., run the 0.80 floor against a sample of historical execution-phase plan reviews) to validate the threshold avoids systematic over-blocking or rubber-stamping before finalizing the value as a protocol constant.
evidence: 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
No spike, probe, or measurement step is described to validate that 0.80 produces acceptable gating behavior on real reviews.
Absence of any historical-distribution analysis or pilot metric in the plan.
findings.finding: The 0.80 execution-phase priority-floor threshold is asserted as a fixed protocol policy with no empirical basis — no measurement plan, historical analysis, or baseline experiment exists to validate that this value avoids systematic over-blocking or rubber-stamping of execution-phase reviews.
findings.location: Settled design → Execution reviews and close gate: 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
scenarios: The 0.80 floor systematically blocks legitimate execution-phase reviews because most blocking criteria for a given domain naturally score below 0.80, creating friction with no quality gain.
The 0.80 floor is too low for the actual risk profile, rubber-stamping execution-phase reviews that should be reconsidered, undermining the policy's intent.
findings.suggested_fix: Before fixing 0.80 in the protocol, run a retrospective analysis: apply the proposed floor to a labeled sample of historical Pass 3 outputs for in-progress tickets and measure (a) fraction of previously-passing reviews that would be newly blocked, (b) fraction of known-problematic reviews that would be caught. Report the distribution; adjust or confirm the value with data. Treat this as a mandatory pre-merge fixture or analysis result.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or explicit task) to capture the key architectural decisions — natural propagation model, signed-pin manifest format, DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract, and plan_review_phase reducer projection — in an ADR, AGENTS.md section, or equivalent persistent design document.
evidence: The plan's 'Health and documentation' section mentions only UI/CLI surfacing and advanced attestation docs for end-users — no ADR or AGENTS.md update is planned.

Multiple novel decisions: natural propagation over eager invalidation (explicitly justified in 'Why this approach'), one-write-lock pre-sign transaction model, DerivedPlanReviewHealth as a new shared contract consumed by every named lifecycle surface, plan_review_phase as a new reducer projection.
Absence of any 'write ADR' or 'update AGENTS.md' acceptance criterion or task.
findings.finding: The plan introduces several novel architectural decisions (signed plan-material pin model, natural-propagation invalidation strategy, DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract, plan_review_phase reducer projection, prerequisite-consistency criterion pipeline) but does not include a task to capture these decisions in an ADR, AGENTS.md, or persistent design document.
findings.location: Settled design (all subsections); Health and documentation section: 'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation.'
scenarios: A future engineer encounters the plan_review_phase projection or DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract without context for why natural propagation was chosen over eager invalidation, leading to re-litigated design decisions or inadvertent violations of the contract.

A rollback or migration scenario requires understanding the manifest evolution strategy (additive lines, numeric floor comparison) but the only authoritative source is this ticket, which may not be discoverable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the plan title and Outcome section to accurately reflect both the advisory-default drift detection model AND the execution-phase/close-gate features, or split the execution-phase machinery into a clearly scoped sub-section with its own stated goal.
evidence: Title: 'Invalidate downstream plan reviews after upstream plan-material changes'
'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins defaults to false. Pin drift remains visible but advisory while disabled; valid remains true.'
The plan introduces plan_review_phase projection, 0.80 execution floor, close gate, and prerequisite consistency review — none implied by the title.
Outcome section describes 'material drift in a pinned ticket invalidates the downstream attestation' but only 'when enforcement is enabled'
findings.finding: The plan title promises 'invalidate downstream plan reviews after upstream plan-material changes,' but the default behavior is advisory drift reporting with opt-in enforcement (enforce_plan_material_pins defaults to false), meaning downstream attestations remain valid by default even after upstream material drift. Additionally, the plan introduces substantial execution-phase machinery (plan_review_phase projection, 0.80 priority floor, require_plan_review_for_close gate, prerequisite consistency review) that goes materially beyond what the title promises.
findings.location: Title / Outcome section / Enforcement and rollout section / Execution reviews and close gate section
scenarios: A reader of the title expects a straightforward invalidation mechanism but finds a multi-feature epic with optional invalidation and a large execution-mode subsystem.
An implementer scoping work from the title would under-estimate the breadth of changes required.
findings.suggested_fix: Retitle to something like 'Add relationship-aware plan-review pins and execution-phase enforcement gates' and update the Outcome to explicitly list all three major deliverables: (1) signed material pins with advisory drift detection, (2) opt-in enforcement invalidating downstream attestations, and (3) execution-phase projection with 0.80 floor and close gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC or scenario in the Health and documentation section that specifies an observable before/after agent workflow comparison or operational validation (e.g., 'an agent attempting to close a task whose prerequisite plan changed and enforcement is enabled receives a directed re-review error naming the drifted pin').
evidence: All 22 ACs are binary system-internal correctness checks.
No AC describes an agent or team member observing a concrete improvement (e.g., 'an agent is blocked from closing a stale-dependency ticket and directed to re-review').
No before/after workflow example or operational metric is provided anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion or plan section provides a concrete validation mechanism — a before/after workflow comparison, operational metric, or dogfooding scenario — that demonstrates observable improvement to the agent or team workflow. All ACs describe internal system-correctness properties (e.g., 'signs pins,' 'survives compaction,' 'projection survives') rather than observable outcomes for the named beneficiary.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section
scenarios: A reviewer cannot confirm the feature delivers value without testing internal system state rather than observing agent/team behavior change.
The absence of an end-to-end scenario makes it hard to validate that the correct gates fire in realistic multi-ticket workflows.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit sequencing note (or dependency edges) among the 7 children that identifies houndish-sapient-fantail as a de-risking prerequisite before building children that depend on its output (enforcement, close gate).
evidence: All 7 children are listed as 'open' with no stated ordering or dependency edges

houndish-sapient-fantail carries the highest technical uncertainty: batching fidelity contract, 'release-gated against a one-prerequisite-per-call gold baseline', recursive bin-splitting on context errors, Pass 2 attribution validation

neutral-depressant-graywolf (pin emission) and convulsive-calx-yearling (enforcement) depend on pin correctness; unpurified-wolflike-flycatcher (close gate) depends on phase and pin health — all implicitly downstream of prerequisite record correctness
Sequencing checklist: 'judge whether a thin vertical-slice / evidence-gated MVP de-risks the riskiest piece first'
findings.finding: The decomposition declares no explicit ordering or dependency edges among the 7 children, and the highest-risk child (houndish-sapient-fantail — LLM-batched prerequisite evaluation with a release-gated fidelity contract) is not identified as a candidate for early de-risking before downstream children that depend on its output (pin emission, close gate, enforcement).
findings.location: Decomposition state / child list; Settled design → Prerequisite consistency review
scenarios: If houndish-sapient-fantail's batched fidelity contract fails its gate test late in the sequence, the close-gate and enforcement children may need rework to accommodate a fall-back one-per-call path
Building the enforcement and close-gate children before validating the prerequisite fidelity contract increases integration risk

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the inbound-blocks prerequisite discovery must include archived tickets (contradicting `_inbound.py`'s current exclusion) and specify any divergence from the existing `find_inbound_relationships` behavior.
evidence: 'Archived tickets remain readable. A pinned target that has been deleted or is missing makes the pin stale and requires relationship repair followed by re-review.'
src/rebar/reducer/_inbound.py line 103: `if state.get('status') in _INACTIVE_SOURCE_STATUSES or state.get('archived'): continue`
The plan's prerequisite definition is 'union of outgoing depends_on targets and inbound blocks sources' — both arms must include archived tickets for the stated semantic to hold.
findings.finding: The plan states 'Archived tickets remain readable' and should be pinnable prerequisites, but the existing inbound-blocks discovery function (`find_inbound_relationships` in `_inbound.py`) explicitly excludes archived tickets (line 103: `if state.get('status') in _INACTIVE_SOURCE_STATUSES or state.get('archived'): continue`). If the prerequisite-collection step reuses this function, archived inbound-blocks sources will be silently omitted from pinning, inconsistent with the plan's stated semantics.
findings.location: Settled design > Material and relationships / 'Archived tickets remain readable' + graph/_blockers.py / reducer/_inbound.py line 103
scenarios: An agent adds a blocks edge from an archived ticket A to subject ticket B. After A is archived, the plan-review of B would not include A in its prerequisite pins, even though A is still 'readable' by the plan's definition. Later consistency checks would miss drift in A's material.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the 'Signed plan-material pins' section specifying that prerequisite discovery uses a dedicated scan that includes archived ticket states (unlike the live-relationship `find_inbound_relationships`), or narrow the 'Archived tickets remain readable' claim to mean only outgoing depends_on targets.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the disposition semantics for a readable prerequisite that has transitioned to closed between pin creation and validity evaluation (e.g., whether closed-state material drift triggers a 'finding' or is treated the same as any other material change).
evidence: 'For a readable pinned prerequisite, consistent and finding are complete evaluations. indeterminate is used only when specialized model evaluation, output parsing, or attribution cannot complete.'
No mention of how the disposition changes when a prerequisite transitions to closed between the original review and the re-evaluation check.

The specialized criterion receives 'the subject plan and the current narrow material of each prerequisite' — but if the prerequisite is now closed, its material may no longer be the material the original review relied on.
findings.finding: The prerequisite consistency review defines three dispositions (consistent / finding / indeterminate) but does not specify the disposition for a prerequisite ticket that was open at review time and has since closed — a case the specialized criterion will encounter on re-evaluation. A closed prerequisite's current material is still computable (the plan says archived/closed states are readable), but 'consistent' vs 'finding' semantics for a newly-closed prerequisite are undefined.
findings.location: Settled design > Prerequisite consistency review / 'For a readable pinned prerequisite, consistent and finding are complete evaluations.'
scenarios: Prerequisite P was open when subject S was reviewed and pinned. P subsequently closes with a changed plan. At the next validity check, P is readable (closed but not deleted). The specialized criterion evaluates S against P's current closed-state material — but the criterion has no guidance on whether a closed prerequisite constitutes a material change (finding) or is inherently consistent (closed = done).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the pre-sign transaction section that 'relationship membership' re-collection includes the full inbound-blocks corpus scan (not only the subject ticket's outgoing deps), and that the resulting HEAD change from any new LINK event is what triggers the revision mismatch and retry.
evidence: 'relationship membership' is listed as one dimension of the pre-sign transaction.
The plan separately states 'This detects new children or inbound blockers globally without reducing tickets under the lock.'
Inbound blocks discovery in the existing codebase (`find_inbound_relationships`, `_find_direct_blockers`) requires scanning all ticket states, not just the subject's `deps` list.
Without explicit specification, an implementer might conflate 'relationship membership' with checking only the subject's own outgoing `deps` array.
findings.finding: The pre-sign re-collection is described as checking 'relationship membership' but the plan does not explicitly state that this includes re-executing the O(corpus) inbound-blocks scan (analogous to `find_inbound_relationships`). Without this explicit requirement, an implementer might re-check only the subject ticket's outgoing deps (O(1)) and miss newly-added inbound `blocks` edges from other tickets, defeating the TOCTOU protection for that relationship dimension.
findings.location: Settled design > Signed plan-material pins / 'The phase story owns one unified pre-sign transaction covering subject material, plan_review_phase, relationship membership, and related fingerprints.'
scenarios: During the pre-LLM snapshot, ticket X has no inbound blocks. Between the snapshot and the pre-sign re-collection, ticket Y adds a `blocks` edge targeting X. If 'relationship membership' re-check only reads X's own `deps` (which doesn't include inbound `blocks` from Y), the change is missed. The HEAD check would catch this only if the implementer correctly includes the inbound scan in the re-collection that determines revision-after.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'Relationship membership re-collection includes a full inbound-blocks scan (equivalent to find_inbound_relationships) to detect newly-added blocks edges from other tickets; any such addition is a git commit that changes HEAD and triggers a retry via the revision mismatch guard.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Prior art' sub-section to 'Signed plan-material pins' explaining why in-toto link attestations / SLSA provenance records (already used in the codebase via DSSE) were evaluated and either found inadequate for cross-ticket manifest embedding or deliberately rejected in favour of the additive-line approach.
evidence: Plan: 'plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>' — a bespoke format recording material dependencies
ADR 0044: 'DSSE is the Sigstore / in-toto / SLSA standard'
ADR 0046: 'in-toto Statement attestations; subject/state binding'
ADR 0049: 'in-toto / SLSA — subject/state binding; non-falsifiable provenance produced by a trusted control plane'
ABSENCE: No section in the plan explains why in-toto link attestations or SLSA provenance records were evaluated and rejected for carrying cross-ticket material pins
findings.finding: The plan introduces a bespoke 'plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-id> <fingerprint>' manifest-line format to record which related-ticket material a review relied on, but never engages with the most directly relevant prior art: in-toto link attestations, which were designed precisely for recording 'which material did this step consume?'. The codebase already uses in-toto Statement shapes throughout (ADR 0044, 0046, 0049 all cite in-toto/SLSA) and the DSSE substrate that wraps them. The plan's 'Why this approach' section justifies staying inside the existing manifest (backward compat, no new storage primitive, additive lines) but does not directly argue why the in-toto link/SLSA provenance model—already present in the codebase—cannot carry these cross-ticket pins, nor why a separate DSSE-enveloped in-toto link per review was rejected.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins — manifest format; Why this approach section
scenarios: An implementer discovering the in-toto link format mid-implementation may redesign the pin format, requiring manifest/parser changes after signing.
A security reviewer may flag the custom format as reinventing in-toto without the existing security analysis, requiring a retroactive prior-art justification.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a paragraph such as: 'In-toto link attestations were considered: they record material inputs per step and ride the existing DSSE substrate. They were rejected here because (a) each pin would require a separate DSSE-enveloped Statement event, introducing the new storage primitive and write amplification this design avoids, and (b) backward-compatible additive manifest lines let pre-feature readers authenticate the whole manifest byte-for-exact-byte while ignoring unknown lines, a property a separate envelope cannot provide.' If this reasoning is correct, codify it; if not, reconsider the format choice.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and register a named Pydantic structured-output contract for the prerequisite consistency criterion's Pass 1 LLM output (analogous to `plan_review_findings`) and specify the Pass 2 verification model extension (the `prerequisite_attribution_valid` field) as a registered contract or extension of the existing `plan_review_verification` contract.
evidence: Plan: 'Pass 1 returns exactly one PrerequisiteCoverageRecord per canonical prerequisite. Its schema is {prerequisite_id: canonical string, disposition: consistent|finding|indeterminate, findings: list[Finding], reason_code?: evaluation-error|output-invalid|attribution-invalid, detail?: string}'
Plan: 'Pass 2 receives the isolated ID map and sets yes only when the indexed original finding names exactly its in-bin canonical ID'

Codebase: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py:213-219 registers plan_review_findings, plan_review_verification, plan_review_novelty, plan_review_completion, plan_review_coach — no prerequisite contract registered
No register_contract call for any prerequisite-criterion output model appears anywhere in the codebase search results
The plan names no Pydantic model (e.g. 'PrerequisiteFinderOutput') and no contract name (e.g. 'plan_review_prerequisite') for the structured call
findings.finding: The prerequisite consistency criterion invokes LLM calls (Pass 1 finder + Pass 2 verifier) but has no named, registered structured-output contract (no Pydantic model, no register_contract call). Every other plan-review pass registers an explicit contract (plan_review_findings, plan_review_verification, etc., all confirmed in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py:213-219). The plan names PrerequisiteCoverageRecord as a schema but never specifies the LLM output model the structured call uses to produce it, leaving the implementor unable to write the structured call without inferring the contract shape.
findings.location: Settled design > Prerequisite consistency review (Pass 1/Pass 2 schema description)
scenarios: Implementor writes the prerequisite LLM calls using runner.run() without a registered structured contract, causing output parsing to fall back to free-form JSON or crash on schema validation
Attribution verification (Pass 2 prerequisite_attribution_valid field) cannot be wired without knowing the output model shape the Pass 1 structured call returns
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Prerequisite consistency review' section: a Pydantic model shape for the Pass 1 output (e.g. PrerequisiteFinderOutput with a coverage: list[PrerequisiteCoverageRecord] field), specify its register_contract name (e.g. 'plan_review_prerequisite'), and specify whether Pass 2 uses a new contract or extends 'plan_review_verification' with an optional prerequisite_attribution_valid field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the aggregation rule that reduces `targets: list[TargetPinDetail]` to the single top-level `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status`, including the full severity ordering (e.g., malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current) and the condition under which legacy-unpinned is emitted.
evidence: Plan: 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth … pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned'

Plan: 'TargetPinDetail is exactly {canonical_id: string, role: child|prerequisite, pinned_fingerprint: string, current_fingerprint: string|null, pin_status: current|stale-pin-drift|stale-pin-missing|malformed-pin}'
Plan: 'targets: list[TargetPinDetail]' — the top-level pin_status must be derived from this list, but no aggregation rule or severity ordering is given anywhere in the plan text
The plan never specifies whether the rule is 'worst wins', 'first non-current wins', or some other ordering
findings.finding: DerivedPlanReviewHealth exposes a single top-level pin_status field (one of: current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, legacy-unpinned) that must be aggregated from the per-target targets: list[TargetPinDetail], each carrying its own pin_status. The plan never specifies the aggregation rule — specifically, the severity ordering used to reduce the list to the single scalar value. Without this rule, two implementations will produce different top-level pin_status values when multiple targets have different per-target statuses (e.g., one stale-pin-drift and one malformed-pin).
findings.location: Settled design > Enforcement and rollout (DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract definition)
scenarios: Ticket has one child with malformed-pin and one prerequisite with stale-pin-drift: is top-level pin_status malformed-pin or stale-pin-drift?
All detail targets show current but legacy-unpinned is also a possible top-level value — when does legacy-unpinned apply if targets list is non-empty?
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the DerivedPlanReviewHealth schema definition: 'pin_status is the worst-severity per-target pin_status, where severity order is malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current; legacy-unpinned is emitted only when no pin lines exist in the manifest (targets list is empty and pins were never written).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the closed enum of `PlanRelationSnapshotError` reason codes (e.g., store-read-failure, dirty-tracker, unmerged-tracker, ambiguous-reference, canonical-id-mismatch) co-located with the exception's definition, and specify which codes are retryable vs terminal.
evidence: Plan: 'A dirty or unmerged tracker is an error, never a comparable revision'
Cached review finding: 'PlanRelationSnapshotError reason codes are referenced as a closed enum throughout the plan and AC/Verification, but their actual vocabulary is never defined'
Plan never enumerates the set of PlanRelationSnapshotError.reason values beyond what can be inferred from individual error scenarios
findings.finding: PlanRelationSnapshotError is referenced as the exception type raised by tracker_head_sha() and collect_plan_relation_snapshot() on failure, and the signing-path error handler keys on it. However, the plan never defines the closed set of valid reason codes for this exception. The only reason code visible in the plan text is 'store-read-failure', but the plan says tracker_head_sha 'rejects dirty or unmerged state' without specifying whether that is the same code or a distinct one, leaving the catch/dispatch logic at the signing path without a complete enum to match against.
findings.location: Settled design > Signed plan-material pins (phase story / pre-sign transaction description)
scenarios: Implementor writes the signing-path handler and must decide whether dirty-state rejection uses reason='store-read-failure' or a distinct reason='dirty-tracker' — the handler's behavior on retry vs abort depends on this
Test authors cannot exhaustively cover PlanRelationSnapshotError reason codes without a defined enum

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly define the manifest line prefix strings for `manifest_review_phase()` and `manifest_priority_floor()` parsers (e.g., `review-phase:` and `priority-floor:`) co-located with the parser specifications, matching the same style as existing `_REGVER_PREFIX`, `_DEP_PREFIX`, etc. constants in manifest.py.
evidence: Plan: 'Add manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), and manifest_pins() parsers beside existing manifest parsers'
Plan: 'The signed manifest line review-phase: has exactly planning or execution. Execution reviews also carry priority-floor: 0.80'

Plan manifest order line: 'optional priority-floor, rebar-version, optional regver, optional disabled_builtins, optional refreshed-from, optional verified-at-sha, zero or more plan-material-pin lines' — review-phase appears before priority-floor

Existing parsers in manifest.py each have an explicit _PREFIX constant (e.g. _REGVER_PREFIX = 'regver:', _DEP_PREFIX = 'dep', _DISABLED_PREFIX = 'disabled_builtins:') — no such constants are specified for the new parsers
findings.finding: The plan introduces two new manifest parsers — manifest_review_phase() and manifest_priority_floor() — but never specifies their manifest line prefix strings co-located with the parser descriptions. The plan gives the line syntax 'review-phase: <value>' and 'priority-floor: <value>' only by implication from usage examples ('The signed manifest line review-phase: has exactly planning or execution'). Unlike plan-material-pin which has explicit format syntax in the plan, the exact prefix tokens for these two parsers are not canonically stated, creating an implementation ambiguity (e.g., 'review-phase:' vs 'review_phase:' vs 'plan-review-phase:').
findings.location: Settled design > Signed plan-material pins (manifest order / parser specification) and Execution reviews
scenarios: Implementor writes manifest_review_phase() using prefix 'review-phase:' but another implementor uses 'plan-review-phase:'; the parsers silently return defaults for each other's lines
Backward-compatibility test fails because the prefix used in the manifest does not match what older readers skip
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the manifest order / parser section: 'The review-phase line prefix is exactly "review-phase:" and the priority-floor line prefix is exactly "priority-floor:"; both follow the same prefix-scan pattern as manifest_regver().'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare explicit inter-sibling ordering constraints: acaf → {75de, 5967, 7c27}; {75de, 5967} → cb6b.
evidence: 75de: 'Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload with the exact review-time PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material records already collected before the LLM by child acaf'
75de AC: 'proves sign_plan_review emits identical complete pin lines with enforcement true and false, using the already-declared dependency on child acaf's emission path'
7c27: 'Consume the pin story's PlanRelationSnapshot contract'
5967: 'Snapshot phase, own material, and PlanRelationSnapshot before the LLM' and 'replaces the direct pre-sign step with the unified atomic snapshot/retry transaction around the already-wired calls'

cb6b: renders 'signed review phase, required phase, and effective execution floor' (5967) and 'canonical related ticket, normalized role, pinned/current fingerprint status' (75de) — both required before cb6b can be complete
No child contains a 'depends_on' or ordering statement naming acaf or the other prerequisite children
findings.finding: 75de-02b0-d454-410b, 5967-4476-7f9f-442d, and 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 each carry semantic implementation dependencies on acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c (PlanRelationSnapshot contract, reviewed_related_material sidecar extension, manifest_pins()), and cb6b-24b4-1056-4ee6 depends on both 75de and 5967 to complete DerivedPlanReviewHealth (pin_status from 75de, phase_status from 5967) before its renderers can be implemented, yet none of these children formally declare these inter-sibling dependencies or specify an integration ordering protocol.
scenarios: If 75de is implemented before acaf, there is no PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material to wire into the sidecar extension
If cb6b is implemented or tested before 5967, DerivedPlanReviewHealth lacks phase_status and rendering of execution-floor/phase metadata is undefined
If 7c27 is implemented before acaf, the typed PlanRelationSnapshot input boundary it consumes does not yet exist
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Depends on' line to each affected child listing its direct sibling prerequisites (acaf for 75de/5967/7c27; 75de+5967 for cb6b); alternatively, note the required integration order in 145e's implementation section so the dogfood story enforces sequencing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate one child (recommended: 75de, as the later story) as the final owner of resign_plan_review's function body, explicitly describing how it incorporates acaf's collect_plan_relation_snapshot call and specifying that the sidecar health guard runs before the collection step.
evidence: acaf: 'Every plan-review signature-producing path—ordinary sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation, and resign_plan_review—calls relation_snapshot.collect_plan_relation_snapshot'
acaf: 'If a referenced target was deleted after the prior PASS, refresh and re-sign return the same unsigned INDETERMINATE missing-target remediation'

75de: 'Edit resign.py: resign_plan_review does not call compute_validity... reads reviewed_related_material, and invokes derive_plan_material_pin_health on those records before signing. With enforcement enabled, stale drift, missing targets, or malformed records refuse re-sign'

Both children list resign_plan_review behavior in their own Acceptance Criteria (acaf AC: 'Re-signing emits the complete current pin set'; 75de AC: 'Enabled pin drift in review-time sidecar records prevents re-sign')
findings.finding: Both acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c and 75de-02b0-d454-410b independently modify resign_plan_review in resign.py — acaf wires in collect_plan_relation_snapshot for current pin emission while 75de refactors the same function to read reviewed_related_material from the sidecar and invoke derive_plan_material_pin_health as a guard — without specifying which child owns the final function shape, the order of operations inside the function, or a merge coordination protocol.
findings.location: children acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c and 75de-02b0-d454-410b
scenarios: If acaf's collection runs before 75de's sidecar guard, an O(n) store scan executes even when enforcement would immediately refuse re-sign — wasted work and potential inconsistency

If 75de's sidecar check and acaf's collection error on missing targets via different code paths (derive_plan_material_pin_health vs PlanRelationSnapshotError), the returned remediation message format may differ depending on which guard fires first
If one child is delivered and the other is not, resign.py is left in an inconsistent intermediate state on the feature branch
findings.suggested_fix: In 75de's implementation section, add a sentence: 'The refactored resign_plan_review runs derive_plan_material_pin_health on sidecar records first; if that guard passes, it then calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot (from acaf) for current pin emission, passing related_material to build_manifest.' Remove the implicit separate wiring from acaf for resign specifically, or mark it as superseded by 75de's edit.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
